key_points: Reflection of light follows two laws: the angle of incidence equals the angle of reflection and the incident ray, reflected ray and normal lie in the same plane
Spherical mirrors are formed from a part of a hollow sphere and are classified as concave or convex mirrors
Important terms related to spherical mirrors include pole, principal axis, centre of curvature, principal focus and focal length
Refraction occurs when light passes from one transparent medium to another due to change in its speed
The refractive index of a medium determines how much the light bends when entering that medium
examples: Concave mirrors used in solar furnaces to concentrate sunlight
Convex mirrors used in security mirrors in shops
A coin appearing raised when seen through water due to refraction
Stars appearing to twinkle due to atmospheric refraction
formulas: Snell's law → n = sin i / sin r
exam_tips: Practice ray diagrams for all positions of object in concave mirrors
Understand sign convention used in mirror formula
Solve numerical problems using mirror formula and magnification
Learn the relationship between refractive index and speed of light
